content:
5 min read
Just now

--

I’ve been building web apps with AI-assisted coding tools across many weekends, and along the way I’ve picked up a handful of tricks that meaningfully changed what a project costs to build and run. Not “use AI faster” tricks. Specific decisions that compounded.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Below three stand out.
1. Match the model to the task
Not every prompt needs a frontier reasoning model.
Scaffolding a CRUD endpoint, renaming variables across a file, writing a migration, fixing an obvious bug — a cheaper, faster model handles all of it fine. Designing a tricky data model, untangling a concurrency issue, or reasoning through a multi-step refactor is where you actually want the heavyweight thinker.
I rotate between models depending on the task. SWE-tuned models for mechanical code work. Kimi and similar for cheaper iteration. The expensive reasoning models only when the problem actually demands reasoning.
And don’t anchor on the popular names. The well-known models are well-known for a reason, but there’s a surprising number of capable smaller and open-weight models that are dirt cheap — sometimes effectively free — and perfectly good at the easy 70% of coding tasks. Worth knowing what’s out there: DeepSeek, Kimi, Qwen, Mistral, and Llama variants are all accessible cheaply or free through providers like Groq, Cerebras, Google AI Studio, and OpenRouter. Spend an afternoon trying a few of them on real work from your own codebase. The cost gap between “good enough for boilerplate” and “the model everyone talks about” is larger than most people realize.
The other lever most people overlook: IDE subscriptions that bundle model access. Windsurf, Cursor, and similar tools include credits across a range of models — including the cheaper tiers — as part of a flat monthly fee. For a solo builder, that often works out cheaper than paying per-token to a single provider, because the subscription absorbs the cost of the experimentation phase where you’re trying models against real tasks to see which one fits. The IDE also handles routing, so switching models is a dropdown instead of a code change. If you’re going to commit to this workflow, the IDE-bundled route is usually the lower-friction starting point.
The principle: model selection is an engineering decision, not a default. Picking the heaviest model for everything burns money on easy work. Picking the lightest for everything burns iterations on hard work. The cost optimum is in the middle, and it’s per-task.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

2. Build a deterministic layer the AI can reference
This is the one that changed the most for me.
When you prompt an AI to build, say, a new API endpoint, you’re implicitly re-specifying the same things every time: how the layers are structured, how errors are shaped, how auth is checked, how data access happens. Every prompt re-derives the same conventions from scratch. That’s expensive in tokens, expensive in attention, and a constant source of drift across the codebase.
The fix: write the conventions down once, in a rules file the IDE references on every prompt.
For example, a rules file for API endpoints might specify:

• Follow REST methodology for routes and verbs

• A controller layer that handles the HTTP request, validates input, and shapes the response

• A business logic layer that exposes its own internal API and contains the actual behavior — callable independently of HTTP

• A data access layer that handles CRUD against the database, with no business logic mixed in

• Errors returned in a consistent envelope

• Tests colocated with each layer following a defined naming convention
That’s already a lot of structure the AI no longer has to guess at. But the single biggest upgrade is including a concrete example inside the rules file — a short, real implementation of one endpoint with the controller, business logic, and data layers wired up the way you want them. One worked example does more to anchor the AI’s output than ten paragraphs of description. The model pattern-matches on the example and produces code that fits the codebase on the first pass instead of the third.
When you prompt “add an endpoint for X,” the AI doesn’t re-invent the structure — it follows the file and mirrors the example. The prompt shrinks. The output is consistent. The review pass is shorter because the AI didn’t have room to drift.
This is the same idea as a coding style guide, but for AI. And the value compounds: once it exists, every feature is cheaper to build than the last.
The natural next step is to generalize it — a portable set of rules for any web SaaS app: auth, endpoints, data access, error handling, tests, migrations. Build it once, carry it across projects.
If you take one thing from this post, take this: the rules file is a cached prompt that pays for itself every time you reference it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

3. Look past the default vendors
The default SaaS stack is well-trodden — SendGrid for email, Twilio for SMS, Stripe for payments, and so on. They work, but the defaults aren’t always optimized for a solo builder shipping a niche product.
Newer entrants often solve adjacent problems the incumbents stopped caring about. My example: I picked Resend over SendGrid for transactional email. Two reasons, and the second one was the bigger surprise.
The pricing is friendlier — Resend’s free tier is generous enough that a solo developer testing in production doesn’t hit a wall. Fine, expected.
But the real win was integration. Resend handled the DNS configuration on my GoDaddy domain without me manually setting up SPF, DKIM, and DMARC records by hand. That’s an evening of fiddly DNS work I didn’t have to do. Newer companies fighting for early adopters tend to invest in exactly this kind of friction removal because it’s how they win against incumbents who’ve stopped caring.
The principle: the default vendor isn’t always the right one for a solo build. Newer entrants compete on the things that hurt most for small teams — onboarding friction, pricing tiers, integration depth — because that’s where they can actually win. Check what shipped in the last year or two before reaching for the obvious name.

Why these matter
Each trick is small on its own. Together they shift the overall cost of the build.
Model selection cuts the cost of every AI interaction. The deterministic layer cuts the cost of every prompt and every review. Smarter vendor choices cut the cost of every external service the app depends on.
None of it is exotic. It’s the kind of optimization that comes from building real things and noticing where the money and time actually go — which, it turns out, isn’t where I expected when I started.
